channel.title: Daily Digest
channel.description: Curated tech blog summaries, inspired by Andrej Karpathy's reading list

item.title: SQLAlchemy 2 In Practice - Chapter 4 - Many-To-Many Relationships
item.description: This article serves as the fourth chapter of Miguel Grinberg's guide on SQLAlchemy 2. It focuses on the implementation and management of many-to-many database relationships.

item.title: Nowhere Is Safe
item.description: The article analyzes how the proliferation of low-cost drones in modern warfare, as seen in Ukraine and Iran, renders traditional air defense systems obsolete. It highlights the strategic shift where massive numbers of inexpensive, asymmetric threats can overwhelm sophisticated, high-cost military defense infrastructure.

item.title: Rapport digitale autonomie binnen de energie-intensieve industrie voor Energy Innovation NL
item.description: Bert Hubert announces the publication of his new report commissioned by Energy Innovation NL, focusing on digital autonomy within energy-intensive industries. The report, developed through consultations with various key companies, addresses the critical integration of technology and industrial energy processes.

item.title: Fewer Computers, Fewer Problems: Going Local With Builds & Deployments
item.description: The author discusses shifting back to local-first builds and deployments for personal websites to simplify the development process. By treating remote hosting as a progressive enhancement rather than a dependency, they aim to eliminate the complexities of distributed computing.

item.title: Hong Kong Disneyland Speedrun Guide
item.description: This guide provides a strategic approach to visiting Hong Kong Disneyland efficiently by leveraging early entry passes and optimized pathing. The author outlines a specific route to maximize ride uptime and minimize queue times, allowing visitors to experience all attractions in half a day.

item.title: Helium Is Hard to Replace
item.description: This article explores the complexities of the global helium supply chain, drawing parallels to how geopolitical conflicts, such as the Strait of Hormuz closure, disrupt essential resource logistics. It highlights why helium is uniquely difficult to substitute, emphasizing its critical role in modern technology and industry.

item.title: Package Security Defenses for AI Agents
item.description: This post highlights the heightened security risks AI agents face when installing software packages, such as malicious code execution and credential theft. It proposes defensive strategies for AI coding platforms, including disabling default install scripts, implementing package version cooldowns, and enforcing sandboxed environments to limit potential blast radii.

item.title: The Great Pyramid of Giza and the Speed of Light
item.description: The author investigates a viral claim that the latitude of the Great Pyramid of Giza matches the speed of light. He concludes that while the numbers align precisely, the observation is a mathematical coincidence rather than evidence of ancient advanced knowledge.

item.title: Random hexagon fractal
item.description: The article explores an iterative geometric process for generating a random fractal based on a hexagon. By repeatedly selecting a side and forming a new triangle with a point inside, the method produces complex, self-similar patterns.

item.title: Root prime gap
item.description: This post discusses Andrica's conjecture, which posits that the difference between the square roots of consecutive prime numbers is always less than one. The author notes that while this remains unproven, it has been empirically verified for primes up to 2 × 10^19.

item.title: A Three- and a Four- Body Problem
item.description: This article discusses the orbital mechanics of the Artemis I and Artemis II missions, focusing on the differences in trajectory and duration. It highlights how the unmanned nature of Artemis I allowed for a more complex, longer-duration orbit compared to the crewed Artemis II mission.

item.title: How do you add or remove a handle from an active Wait­For­Multiple­Objects?
item.description: The article explains that it is impossible to directly modify the handles being monitored by an active WaitForMultipleObjects call. Instead, the author advises using a cooperative approach between threads to manage the monitored objects.

item.title: I quit drinking for a year
item.description: The author recounts their experience of quitting alcohol for over a year after a spontaneous decision triggered by a discussion about 'damp January.' They discovered that total abstinence was surprisingly easier than moderate drinking because it removed the mental burden of constantly deciding whether or not to consume alcohol.

item.title: Book Review: Small Comfort by Ia Genberg ★★☆☆☆
item.description: Terence Eden reviews the book 'Small Comfort' by Ia Genberg, expressing mixed feelings about its execution. While he appreciates the experimental structure of interrelated stories, he finds the overall narrative unconvincing and disjointed.

item.title: Pluralistic: Cindy Cohn's "Privacy's Defender" (09 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow highlights the memoir of EFF executive director Cindy Cohn, 'Privacy's Defender,' which chronicles her decades-long fight for digital rights. The post focuses on her pivotal role in the landmark Bernstein case, which established that code constitutes protected free speech.

item.title: What Are You Trying to Say?
item.description: The article explores the challenge of clear communication and the tendency to overcomplicate messages. It suggests that asking oneself 'what am I trying to say?' helps strip away unnecessary fluff, encouraging writers and speakers to distill complex thoughts into their core, primitive meaning.

item.title: GitHub Repo Size
item.description: Simon Willison has released a simple web tool that displays the total size of any GitHub repository. It bypasses the lack of size information in the GitHub UI by leveraging the repository's CORS-friendly API.

item.title: asgi-gzip 0.3
item.description: Simon Willison released version 0.3 of asgi-gzip to fix an issue where SSE responses were being incorrectly compressed. The update was triggered after realizing that the library's automated sync with Starlette had failed to capture a necessary upstream fix.

item.title: Meta's new model is Muse Spark, and meta.ai chat has some interesting tools
item.description: Meta has released Muse Spark, a new hosted AI model available via meta.ai with 'Instant' and 'Thinking' modes. The author tests the model's capabilities using his signature 'pelican riding a bicycle' prompt to compare performance across these modes.

item.title: When the crisis comes
item.description: Anil Dash reflects on the challenges of leadership during constant crises, emphasizing the importance of finding clarity and expert support. He highlights the book 'Crisis Engineering' as a vital resource for navigating turbulent times and transforming crises into opportunities for growth.

item.title: The day you get cut out of the economy
item.description: The article argues that as global economic growth stagnates, AI frontier model developers will shift from a SaaS subscription model to vertical integration. By capturing the value currently held by the AI application layer, model makers will consolidate the market to recoup the massive costs of frontier training runs.

item.title: Pork & Puppetry
item.source: Tedium: The Dull Side of the Internet.
item.description: This article explores the origin of a viral parody trailer featuring a warthog puppet named Pork Johnson, which mocks the open-source image editor GIMP. The author interviews creator Dustin Grissom, who explains how the puppet served as a creative outlet and a personal tribute to his childhood.

item.title: Package Security Problems for AI Agents
item.description: This article examines how AI agents are uniquely vulnerable to traditional package security threats like typosquatting and registry poisoning. Because agents dynamically discover and install tools without human oversight, they can inadvertently execute malicious code from compromised or spoofed package repositories.

item.title: Mario and Earendil
item.description: Armin Ronacher announces that Mario Zechner is joining Earendil. The post highlights Ronacher's admiration for Zechner's commitment to software quality, thoughtful design, and craftsmanship in the field of AI coding agents.

item.title: How do you add or remove a handle from an active Msg­Wait­For­Multiple­Objects?
item.description: The article explains that you cannot directly add or remove a handle from an active MsgWaitForMultipleObjects call. Instead, you must design your application logic to signal the waiter to handle these updates internally.

item.title: Theatre Review: Avenue Q ★★★★★
item.description: The author reflects on re-watching the musical Avenue Q, contemplating the passage of time and the challenges of updating classic productions. The review highlights the emotional resonance of the show while discussing the balance between preserving a work's original charm and keeping it relevant.

item.title: Pluralistic: Process knowledge (08 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: This article examines the problematic nature of the term 'intellectual property,' arguing that it is a vague, catch-all label for disparate legal doctrines and abstract concepts. It highlights how the conflation of copyright, patents, trademarks, and newer, less-defined rights complicates modern tech regulation and global trade policy.

item.title: Quoting Giles Turnbull
item.description: This post highlights a reflection by Giles Turnbull on the double standard regarding AI adoption. It notes that while people enjoy using AI to mimic other professions, they often resent when AI is used to replace their own work.

item.title: Actually, people love to work hard
item.description: Anil Dash challenges the common corporate trope that employees lack a desire to work hard. He argues that people are inherently motivated when they have clear goals, shared values, and trust, suggesting that claims of laziness are merely management tactics to suppress wages.

item.title: Weekly Update 498
item.description: Troy Hunt shares his frustrations regarding the administrative burden of chasing long-overdue invoices from clients. The update highlights the real-world difficulties of managing payment terms in a professional consulting business.

item.title: Michael Nielsen – How science actually progresses
item.description: Michael Nielsen discusses the nuances of scientific progress, challenging traditional narratives about historical figures like Einstein, Newton, and Darwin. The conversation explores how scientific breakthroughs actually occur through collaborative environments and evolving methodologies rather than isolated genius.

item.title: Readership maths skills
item.description: This article discusses the importance of mathematical literacy for readers to better understand data-driven narratives. It highlights how improved analytical skills help audiences evaluate information more critically.

item.title: Who Built This?
item.description: This article explores how different programming ecosystems—Go, Rust, .NET, and Java—handle embedding version control metadata into compiled binaries. It highlights Go's automatic VCS stamping as a gold standard for observability and contrasts it with the varied, often manual, opt-in approaches required in other languages.

item.title: The Hacker News tarpit
item.description: This post discusses the author's subscription model for their newsletter, highlighting the benefits of paid access such as additional content and community interaction. It emphasizes the author's commitment to keeping the core content free while offering premium perks for supporters.

item.title: Toffoli gates are all you need
item.description: The article discusses Landauer’s principle, which establishes a fundamental thermodynamic limit on the energy required to erase a bit of information. It highlights the universality of this physical bound regardless of the storage medium and introduces the role of Toffoli gates in reversible computation.

item.title: Were there any Windows 3.1 programs that were so incompatible with Windows 95 that there was no point trying to patch them?
item.description: This post discusses the history of software compatibility during the transition from Windows 3.1 to Windows 95. It specifically addresses whether certain programs were fundamentally incompatible beyond the possibility of patching.

item.title: The Building Block Economy
item.description: This article explores the concept of the 'Building Block Economy' within the context of software architecture and infrastructure development. It discusses how modular, reusable components form the foundation for scalable and efficient systems.

item.title: Did WordPress VIP leak my phone number?
item.description: The author investigates how his private phone number was leaked by Apollo.io, tracing the source back to Parse.ly (acquired by WordPress VIP). The post highlights concerns regarding data privacy and the practice of sharing user information within contributor networks.

item.title: Pluralistic: Switzerland's Goldilocks fiber (07 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: This article discusses the success of Switzerland's high-speed fiber internet model, which provides 25Gbit symmetrical connections via a competitive, public-provisioned structure. The author contrasts this 'Goldilocks' approach with the flawed telecommunications markets in the US and Germany, arguing that robust public infrastructure is essential for true competition.

item.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
item.description: Russian military intelligence hackers are exploiting vulnerabilities in older routers to steal Microsoft Office authentication tokens. This campaign has compromised over 18,000 networks by silently harvesting tokens without the need for installing malicious software.

item.title: GLM-5.1: Towards Long-Horizon Tasks
item.description: Simon Willison tests the new 754B parameter GLM-5.1 model, highlighting its impressive SVG generation capabilities. While the model shows potential in complex tasks, it demonstrates mixed results when automatically generating CSS animations for those assets.

item.title: Anthropic's Project Glasswing - restricting Claude Mythos to security researchers - sounds necessary to me
item.description: Anthropic has introduced Project Glasswing to restrict access to their powerful new AI model, Claude Mythos, due to its advanced cybersecurity capabilities. By limiting availability to select partners, Anthropic aims to allow the industry time to address the significant vulnerabilities the model can identify before a wider release.

item.title: SQLite WAL Mode Across Docker Containers Sharing a Volume
item.description: This research confirms that SQLite's Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) mode functions correctly across separate Docker containers when sharing the same host volume. It explains that because containers on the same host share the underlying filesystem and memory mechanisms, concurrent SQLite processes can collaborate effectively.

item.title: What next for the compute crunch?
item.description: The AI sector is facing a severe imbalance between exponential demand for compute power and limited supply. The next two years will be characterized by market shortages, supply rationing, and ongoing price adjustments.

item.title: Prototyping with LLMs
item.description: The author reflects on the pitfalls of diving too quickly into LLM-based prototyping without proper planning. They argue that sketching ideas on paper before using AI can be a more efficient way to validate concepts and save time.

item.title: I Tried Vibing an RSS Reader and My Dreams Did Not Come True
item.description: The author explores the potential and limitations of 'vibe coding,' using AI to build a custom RSS reader without deep technical knowledge. While the project successfully produced a functional tool, the author highlights the challenges of scaling AI-generated code and the eventual wall hit when the application complexity exceeds AI's assistance capabilities.

item.title: The Cathedral and the Catacombs
item.description: The author critiques the long-standing 'Cathedral and the Bazaar' metaphor in software development, arguing that it ignores the 'catacombs'—the often-overlooked, complex dependency graphs that form the foundation of modern projects. The piece suggests that by focusing solely on governance and construction processes, developers and researchers fail to account for the risky, unmaintained, and invisible codebases that support their work.

item.title: HIPAA compliant AI
item.description: The article suggests that running AI models locally on private hardware is the most effective method for maintaining HIPAA compliance when handling protected health information. While HIPAA-compliant cloud alternatives exist, they are often characterized as overly restrictive and costly.

item.title: Learning to read C++ compiler errors: Illegal use of -> when there is no -> in sight
item.description: This post explains how to debug confusing C++ compiler errors by examining code you didn't write, such as headers or macros. It emphasizes that unexpected error messages often stem from hidden code being expanded during compilation.

item.title: [RSS Club] Banana for scale
item.description: The author introduces a creative project called 'Scan Slowly And See' which uses a unique QR code generated by cloning patterns from a banana's skin. This playful experiment invites readers to test the functionality of the custom-coded graphic.

item.title: Pluralistic: Your boss wants to use surveillance data to cut your wages (06 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: This article explores how companies use surveillance data to implement 'personalized pricing,' a strategy that allows businesses to extract maximum profit from consumers and devalue labor. The author argues that this practice, fueled by monopolies and regulatory capture, is a key component of 'enshittification' in the digital economy.

item.title: AI Did It in 12 Minutes. It Took Me 10 Hours to Fix It
item.description: The author explores the tension between using AI to accelerate coding tasks and the importance of maintaining a deep understanding of one's codebase. Despite AI's ability to generate code quickly, the author emphasizes that developers must retain a mental model of their projects to ensure long-term maintainability and successful debugging.

item.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
item.description: German authorities have identified 31-year-old Russian national Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin as the notorious hacker 'UNKN'. He is alleged to have led the REvil and GandCrab ransomware gangs, responsible for over 130 extortion attacks in Germany between 2019 and 2021.

item.title: Google AI Edge Gallery
item.description: Simon Willison reviews the Google AI Edge Gallery app, which allows users to run Gemma 4 models directly on an iPhone. The app demonstrates impressive performance and features, including image analysis, audio transcription, and a unique 'skills' system for tool calling.

item.title: datasette-ports 0.2
item.description: Datasette-ports 0.2 has been released, now allowing the tool to run independently via uvx without requiring a full Datasette installation. It remains compatible as a Datasette plugin, preserving the existing command-line functionality.

item.title: scan-for-secrets 0.3
item.description: Simon Willison released version 0.3 of scan-for-secrets, a tool for identifying and handling sensitive information. This update introduces a redaction feature via both the command line interface and a new Python function.

item.title: Cleanup Claude Code Paste
item.description: This post introduces a specialized web tool designed to clean up text copied from the Claude Code terminal. It automatically removes the command prompt symbol, fixes broken line wrapping, and tidies up whitespace for cleaner output.

item.title: datasette-ports 0.1
item.description: Simon Willison has released datasette-ports 0.1, a new tool designed to help developers manage multiple running Datasette instances. It provides a simple command-line interface to list active local instances, including their associated databases and installed plugins.

item.title: Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI
item.description: Lalit Maganti successfully built 'syntaqlite,' a robust SQLite parsing and linting tool, after years of hesitation by leveraging AI coding agents to handle the tedious grammar implementation. The project demonstrates how AI can help developers overcome procrastination and technical hurdles by facilitating rapid prototyping and concrete problem-solving.

item.title: Stamp It! All Programs Must Report Their Version
item.description: The author argues that inadequate software versioning leads to significant delays during production incident responses. He proposes a three-step framework—Stamp it, Plumb it, and Report it—to ensure all programs provide transparent version information.

item.title: Someone at BrowserStack is Leaking Users' Email Address
item.description: The author details a security incident where they discovered their unique email address, created specifically for BrowserStack, was leaked. By using domain-specific email aliases, they were able to trace the source of the breach and identify unauthorized usage.

item.title: It's not that deep
item.description: The author reflects on the value of reading personal, human-centric blogs as a way to find connection and inspiration beyond the pressure of constant productivity. By highlighting specific writers, the post encourages readers to seek out authentic voices rather than sterile, corporate content.

item.title: Quoting Chengpeng Mou
item.description: This post shares data from OpenAI's Chengpeng Mou regarding ChatGPT usage patterns in the U.S. It highlights significant engagement with health-related inquiries, particularly among users in hospital deserts and outside of standard clinical hours.

item.title: Syntaqlite Playground
item.description: The Syntaqlite Playground provides a browser-based interface to test SQL formatting, parsing, validation, and tokenization. Developed using Pyodide and WebAssembly, this tool allows users to interact directly with the syntaqlite library in their browser.

item.title: scan-for-secrets 0.2
item.description: The scan-for-secrets tool has been updated to version 0.2, introducing streaming results for better performance on large directories. It also adds support for scanning multiple directories or individual files, along with new Python API functions for improved developer integration.

item.title: scan-for-secrets 0.1.1
item.description: Simon Willison released version 0.1.1 of the scan-for-secrets tool. This update includes documentation for supported escaping schemes and removes redundant code.

item.title: scan-for-secrets 0.1
item.description: Simon Willison has released scan-for-secrets, a Python tool designed to detect API keys and sensitive credentials within local directories. It supports advanced features like scanning for common encodings and allows users to define custom configurations to automate secret retrieval during scans.

item.title: research-llm-apis 2026-04-04
item.description: Simon Willison has released a new repository containing research on raw API interactions with various LLM providers to inform updates to his LLM Python library. By utilizing Claude Code to analyze client libraries and generate curl commands, the project aims to improve support for advanced features like server-side tool execution.

item.title: Value numbering
item.description: This article introduces value numbering as a compiler optimization technique that builds upon Static Single Assignment (SSA) form. It explains how identifying identical expressions allows compilers to eliminate redundant computations by reusing previously calculated values.

item.title: Wander Console 0.4.0
item.description: Wander Console 0.4.0 is a release of a decentralized, self-hosted web tool for exploring independent websites. The update introduces wildcard patterns for ignore lists, a 'via' query parameter for attribution, and improvements to the console picker algorithm.

item.title: Reading List 04/04/2026
item.description: This post provides a curated collection of industry updates and reading materials focusing on supply chain disruptions, infrastructure challenges, and emerging market trends. Topics include aluminum market issues, the impact of electric vehicles on traditional manufacturing hubs, and SpaceX's potential public offering.

item.title: What does Open Source mean?
item.description: This article explores the confusion surrounding the term 'open source,' noting that debates often fail because participants define it differently. It breaks down the term into four distinct categories: a licensing regime, a development methodology, a business model, and a supply chain.

item.title: The AI writing witchhunt is pointless.
item.description: The article argues against the backlash toward AI writing by comparing it to the historical collaborative model of Alexandre Dumas. It highlights that literary greats often relied on ghostwriters and assistants, suggesting that the current resistance to AI overlooks the long-standing nature of assisted writing.

item.title: Kalman and Bayes average grades
item.description: This article explains how calculating a rolling average grade serves as a foundational example for understanding Bayesian statistics and Kalman filtering. It demonstrates how incoming data points update previous estimates, highlighting the underlying mathematical connections between these concepts.

item.title: Absurd In Production
item.description: This post reflects on the five-month production experience of Absurd, a durable execution system built entirely on top of PostgreSQL. The author discusses system refinements, including enhanced claim handling, deadlock prevention, and improved workflow step management, confirming the viability of using SQL-based storage for long-running tasks.

item.title: Welcome to RSS Club!
item.description: The author introduces 'RSS Club', an exclusive series of blog posts accessible only to RSS/Atom subscribers. These posts are hidden from search engines and social media, creating a private space for long-form readers.

item.title: Loading... [13 kB]
item.description: This article explains how the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) ensures data integrity when downloading files over the internet. It details the process of packet sequencing and acknowledgment to handle out-of-order delivery and packet loss effectively.

item.title: Pluralistic: EU ready to cave to Trump on tech (04 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow critiques the EU's potential failure to regulate US tech giants amidst geopolitical pressures under the Trump administration. The piece argues that the EU should capitalize on current crises to address American digital surveillance, monopolistic data practices, and the weaponization of software against public institutions.

item.title: Quoting Kyle Daigle
item.description: This post highlights GitHub's rapid platform growth, citing data from COO Kyle Daigle. It specifically notes a massive increase in commit activity and the doubling of GitHub Actions usage minutes.

item.title: Vulnerability Research Is Cooked
item.description: Thomas Ptacek argues that AI coding agents are poised to revolutionize vulnerability research by automating exploit discovery. By leveraging vast pattern-matching capabilities and relentless search processes, these models can identify zero-day vulnerabilities more efficiently than traditional methods.

item.title: The cognitive impact of coding agents
item.description: Simon Willison shares a viral short-form video clip from his appearance on Lenny Rachitsky's podcast. The post explores the cognitive impact of using coding agents and touches on the broader implications of AI engineering.

item.title: The Mystery of Rennes-le-Château, Part 3: A Secret History
item.description: This article explores the historical and pseudo-historical origins of Rennes-le-Château, focusing on the investigation by Henry Lincoln. It serves as part of a series examining the real-world research that inspired the game Gabriel Knight 3.

item.title: The Reckoning
item.description: The author reflects on the societal 'reckoning' brought about by AI, criticizing how its marketing has focused on existential threats and displacement rather than utility. The post contrasts the hyper-individualism of the US with the communal cohesion observed in Hong Kong, arguing for structural improvements to restore social trust.

item.title: Em Dashes: Back In Style?
item.source: Tedium: The Dull Side of the Internet.
item.description: The author reflects on their long-term struggle to maintain a massive, legacy WordPress blog archive. They explore Cloudflare's new 'EmDash' tool, which aims to simplify migrating unstable WordPress sites to the modern Astro framework.

item.title: Package Manager Easter Eggs
item.description: This article explores the long-standing tradition of hidden easter eggs within various software package managers. It showcases specific examples, such as the 'moo' commands in apt, aptitude, and zypper, which reveal creative ASCII art and playful interactions.

item.title: The "Passive Income" trap ate a generation of entrepreneurs
item.description: The article critiques the trend of entrepreneurs pursuing 'passive income' through low-value ventures like dropshipping. It suggests that many such business models lack genuine innovation and long-term sustainability.

item.title: Roman moon, Greek moon
item.description: The article explains the terminology used for the closest approach to the moon, noting the interchangeability of 'perilune' and 'periselene'. It clarifies that the terms are derived from Roman and Greek roots for the moon, respectively.

item.title: Hyperbolic version of Napier’s mnemonic
item.description: The author explores a hyperbolic analog to Napier's mnemonic, a tool traditionally used in spherical trigonometry. While noting that hyperbolic analogs exist for most geometric concepts, the author highlights this specific discovery as a unique mathematical curiosity.

item.title: How can I use Read­Directory­ChangesW to know when someone is copying a file out of the directory?
item.description: This post clarifies that file copying is not a distinct file system operation, making it impossible to detect directly via ReadDirectoryChangesW. It explains that since copying is just a series of read and write operations, the file system cannot distinguish it from other file access patterns.

item.title: Book Review: Superintelligence - Paths, Dangers, Strategies by Nick Bostrom ★★★★⯪
item.description: This review discusses Nick Bostrom's 2014 book, Superintelligence, highlighting its profound analysis of the existential risks associated with true artificial intelligence. The author emphasizes the book's foresight in outlining necessary safety measures and conceptual frameworks long before current AI advancements.

item.title: My Zip bomb strategy is not as effective as it used to be
item.description: The author discusses the declining effectiveness of using zip bombs to neutralize malicious web bots. While previously a reliable defense for a modest server setup, the author notes that modern, more sophisticated bots are increasingly able to circumvent this mitigation strategy.

item.title: Quoting Willy Tarreau
item.description: Willy Tarreau reports a massive increase in Linux kernel security vulnerability reports, rising from a few per week to 5-10 per day. This surge is largely attributed to AI tools, which have improved detection efficiency to the point of generating frequent duplicate findings.

item.title: Quoting Daniel Stenberg
item.description: Daniel Stenberg, the lead developer of cURL, discusses the changing impact of AI on open source security. He notes a shift from an influx of AI-generated 'slop' to a high volume of legitimate, high-quality security reports that require significant manual review.

item.title: Quoting Greg Kroah-Hartman
item.description: Linux kernel maintainer Greg Kroah-Hartman notes a significant shift in AI-generated security reports. Initially dismissed as low-quality 'slop,' AI tools are now producing accurate and actionable security analysis for open-source projects.

item.title: Can JavaScript Escape a CSP Meta Tag Inside an Iframe?
item.description: This post investigates whether JavaScript can bypass Content Security Policy (CSP) meta tags injected within sandboxed iframes. The author demonstrates that these meta tags are effectively enforced by the browser even when subsequent untrusted JavaScript attempts to modify them.

item.title: The Axios supply chain attack used individually targeted social engineering
item.description: Axios maintainers released a postmortem detailing a sophisticated supply chain attack that targeted a developer through highly personalized social engineering. The attackers impersonated company leadership and used fake professional environments to trick the maintainer into installing malware during a video conference.

item.title: Build your own Dial-up ISP with a Raspberry Pi
item.description: This article explores the technical history of the iBook G3 and its significance as the first consumer laptop with built-in Wi-Fi. It sets the stage for a project involving legacy hardware connectivity and the creation of a dial-up ISP using a Raspberry Pi.

item.title: Programming (with AI agents) as theory building
item.description: This article explores the concept of programming not as mere instruction-giving, but as the active construction of mental models and theories. It suggests that integrating AI agents into the development process shifts the developer's role toward architecting these theoretical frameworks.

item.title: SQLAlchemy 2 In Practice - Chapter 3 - One-To-Many Relationships
item.description: This article serves as the third chapter of Miguel Grinberg's book on SQLAlchemy 2, focusing on implementing one-to-many relationships. It follows a previous discussion on product queries, transitioning into managing data associations between entities.

item.title: The Blandness of Systematic Rules vs. The Delight of Localized Sensitivity
item.description: This post explores the tension between adhering to rigid systematic UI design rules and the value of localized, context-aware design choices. The author argues that while systematic rules promote consistency, thoughtful exceptions can provide a level of delight and clarity that universal guidelines sometimes stifle, especially as software scales.
